description: Common interview questions for technology-related categories.

questions.question: When something is not working in a technical project, what is usually the best first step?

choices.text: Randomly change multiple things until the issue disappears.
Reproduce the issue and identify when and where it happens.
Delete the feature immediately.
Assume the tool or framework is broken.

questions.correctExplanation: Reproducing the issue helps you understand the problem clearly before making changes.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Random changes can create new bugs and make the root cause harder to find.
Deleting the feature is usually too extreme and does not help you learn the cause.
Framework or tool issues can happen, but application-level mistakes are more common.

questions.question: Why is documentation useful in a technical team?

choices.text: It helps team members understand decisions, setup steps, and how systems work.
It replaces the need to test the product.
It makes bugs impossible.
It should only be written after a project is abandoned.

questions.correctExplanation: Good documentation helps people onboard, maintain systems, and understand past decisions.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Documentation supports testing but does not replace it.
Documentation can reduce confusion, but it cannot make bugs impossible.
Documentation is most useful while a project is active and evolving.

questions.question: What is one important benefit of using version control such as Git?

choices.text: It tracks changes and helps teams collaborate safely on the same project.
It automatically writes all project code.
It removes the need for communication.
It only works for design files, not code.

questions.correctExplanation: Version control records changes, supports collaboration, and makes it easier to review or revert work.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Git tracks and manages code changes, but it does not automatically write the code.
Teams still need communication even when using version control.
Git is widely used for code and can also track many other file types.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of testing technical work?

choices.text: To prove that no future bugs will ever happen.
To check that important behavior works as expected and reduce regression risk.
To avoid understanding the system.
To make the project look more complicated.

questions.correctExplanation: Tests help confirm expected behavior and reduce the chance of breaking existing functionality during changes.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Tests reduce risk but cannot guarantee that no bugs will ever happen.
Testing supports understanding, but it does not replace understanding.
Good tests should clarify behavior, not add unnecessary complexity.

questions.question: You receive unclear technical requirements. What should you do before starting implementation?

choices.text: Start building immediately and hope your assumptions are correct.
Ask clarifying questions and confirm the expected outcome.
Ignore the requirements and build what you prefer.
Wait silently until someone gives more details.

questions.correctExplanation: Clarifying requirements helps avoid wasted work and ensures the solution matches the actual goal.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Starting with unclear assumptions can lead to rework.
Building your own preferred solution may not solve the real problem.
Waiting silently delays progress and does not resolve uncertainty.

questions.question: Why should sensitive information such as API keys not be committed to a public repository?

choices.text: Because exposed secrets can be stolen and used to access systems or create costs.
Because API keys make code run slower.
Because repositories cannot store text files.
Because secrets are only useful in frontend styling.

questions.correctExplanation: Secrets can grant access to services, data, or paid APIs, so exposing them can create security and financial risks.

wrongExplanations.explanation: The main issue is security, not performance.
Repositories can store text files, but sensitive values should not be exposed.
Secrets are usually used for authentication or service access, not styling.

questions.question: A user says an app feels slow. What is a good first response from a technical perspective?

choices.text: Measure where the delay happens before choosing a solution.
Rewrite the entire application immediately.
Ignore the report because performance is subjective.
Add more features to distract users.

questions.correctExplanation: Measuring performance helps identify whether the issue is network, rendering, database, client-side code, or another cause.

wrongExplanations.explanation: A full rewrite is risky and unnecessary before understanding the problem.
User reports can reveal real performance issues and should be investigated.
Adding features does not solve the performance problem.

questions.question: A technical issue appears only in production, not locally. What is the best approach?

choices.text: Compare environment differences, logs, data, configuration, and deployment behavior.
Assume users are mistaken.
Disable production monitoring.
Make random production changes until it works.

questions.correctExplanation: Production-only issues often come from environment variables, data differences, build settings, network conditions, or deployment configuration.

wrongExplanations.explanation: User reports should be investigated, especially when production behavior differs.
Monitoring helps diagnose production issues and should not be disabled.
Random production changes are risky and can create new problems.

questions.question: In a technical interview, what is a strong way to discuss a trade-off you made?

choices.text: Explain the options, why you chose one, what risk it had, and how you managed that risk.
Say your choice had no downside.
Avoid discussing trade-offs because they sound negative.
Blame the team for any limitation.

questions.correctExplanation: Strong technical judgment includes understanding options, constraints, risks, and mitigation strategies.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Most real technical decisions involve trade-offs, so saying there was no downside may sound unrealistic.
Discussing trade-offs can show maturity and problem-solving ability.
Blaming others does not demonstrate ownership or judgment.

questions.question: What is a good way to explain how you learned a new technical tool?

choices.text: Mention the goal, the resources you used, what you built or practiced, and what you learned.
Say you learned it instantly without any practice.
Say you only copied examples without understanding them.
Avoid details because technical learning is too hard to explain.

questions.correctExplanation: A strong answer shows learning process, initiative, and practical application.

wrongExplanations.explanation: This sounds unrealistic and does not show your learning process.
Copying without understanding does not demonstrate skill growth.
Specific details make your answer more credible.
